text: Fun device. I think when you have a few different thresholds on that for a few contacts it would be hard to reproduce and certainly obsoleted by modern electronics. author: @Graham_Wideman
text:
For those interested, the device demonstrated in this video is akin to a variety of commercially available relay called a "latching relay".  That kind of relay has magnets that can latch the contacts in either of two positions while no power is applied. You can force the relay to one or other state by applying a voltage to one or other of two control coils. Or some varieties have a single coil and you apply a voltage with one polarity or the other.  
These are extremely useful in situations where the relay may spend a long time in either of the states, so with a normal relay there's a 50% chance it's going to be consuming power and producing heat just to hold in the "active" position.  With the latching relay, the controlling circuit just applies a brief pulse one way or the other to get the relay to the new desired position. Latching relays are used, for example, in test equipment, to select input range.  Test equipment often needs to be designed to minimize internal temperature drift, and avoiding heat-emitting relays helps with that goal.

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
I think you're on to something here.
This way one could build a very simple low-budget charge-controller for a windturbine (or such), if you make the potentiometer control the amount power made by the windturbine going to the battery. It could also stop charging if there's not enough voltage to charge the battery anyway.

Maybe not the most efficient charge-controller according to some people, but very very cheap and without electronic abracadabra.

author: @ChuckYoung75
text: Another great variation is something i made for a personal project. Mine was a little different than your setup, but changing it to match yours... id put magnets on the saw blade sandwiched. Then 2 small coils in series placed one on each side of the sandwiched magnets. Then a short positive pulse will swing it to connect and hold until a short negative pulse is sent through it and it will flip and hold the other way. I actually thought of patenting it years ago but thats a pain. But its a darn sweet relay that takes nearly nothing to flip it either way...

replies.author: @deslomeslager
replies.text: with only one coil is the same, just need a negative voltage source. I love self latching relays. You need only one peak of current, just once.

replies.author: @ChuckYoung75
replies.text: @deslomeslager  I agree. I just did 2 so I got the push pull from both sides at the same time. Just takes less energy to flip it and latch. Works great, and simple.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: What you have there is actually the basis of the old analog meter where the position of the arm/ needle is based on the strength of the magnetic field  which in turn is based on the current flowing through the  coil. If you put a calibrated display panel behind the arm we can tell the current going through the  coil by looking  at the display behind it. Obviously voltage is read as the coil has resistance which which limits / controls the current going through the coil.
